name: Sword of Damocles
description: The Sword of Damocles is not a weapon of supernatural power or legendary craftsmanship, but rather a potent symbol of the precariousness of power and the constant threat that accompanies even the most enviable positions. It hangs suspended by a single horsehair over the head of Damocles, a courtier of Dionysius I of Syracuse, in a moral lesson illustrating the burdens and fears inherent in leadership. The sword itself, likely a standard weapon of the time, is rendered extraordinary not by its physical properties but by its context. It serves as a constant reminder of the dangers, responsibilities, and anxieties that plague those in positions of authority. The tale, often retold and reinterpreted, explores themes of mortality, responsibility, and the deceptive nature of happiness, suggesting that outward appearances of success often mask inner turmoil and the ever-present potential for disaster. The sword is a metaphor for the anxieties of leadership and the ever-present threat of downfall.
subtitle: A symbol of impending doom and the precariousness of power.
origin: The sword was placed above Damocles' head by Dionysius I of Syracuse to teach him a lesson about the responsibilities and dangers of being a ruler.
currentLocation: Figurative; exists in the realm of moral allegory and literature.

keyMyths.title: The Banquet of Dionysius
keyMyths.description: Dionysius, tyrant of Syracuse, grew weary of Damocles' constant flattery and envious remarks about his power. To teach him a lesson, Dionysius invited Damocles to a lavish banquet and seated him on a golden couch beneath a sharp sword suspended by a single horsehair. This experience revealed to Damocles the true nature of power, which is accompanied by constant fear and responsibility.

keyMyths.title: The Price of Power
keyMyths.description: The story highlights the inherent dangers of holding a position of power. While rulers may enjoy wealth and prestige, they are also constantly vulnerable to enemies, betrayal, and the weight of their responsibilities. The sword symbolizes the ever-present threat of downfall and the anxieties that accompany leadership.
keyMyths.source: Various philosophical interpretations and adaptations of the original story.

keyMyths.title: Modern Interpretations
keyMyths.description: The phrase 'Sword of Damocles' is frequently used in modern language to describe any situation where someone is in a position of power or success but is also constantly threatened by some impending disaster or negative outcome. It is a powerful metaphor for anxiety and the precarious nature of good fortune.
keyMyths.source: Common usage in political discourse and contemporary literature.

sources.author: Cicero
sources.description: The primary source for the story of the Sword of Damocles. Cicero uses the anecdote to illustrate a philosophical point about the nature of happiness and the burdens of power.
sources.text: The primary source for the story of the Sword of Damocles. Cicero uses the anecdote to illustrate a philosophical point about the nature of happiness and the burdens of power.

sources.author: Plutarch
sources.description: While Plutarch does not directly recount the story of Damocles, his biographies of rulers often explore similar themes of power, responsibility, and the potential for corruption.
sources.text: While Plutarch does not directly recount the story of Damocles, his biographies of rulers often explore similar themes of power, responsibility, and the potential for corruption.

sources.author: Leach, Eleanor Winsor
sources.description: A modern scholarly analysis of Cicero's work, providing context and interpretation of the Damocles story within its broader philosophical framework.
sources.text: A modern scholarly analysis of Cicero's work, providing context and interpretation of the Damocles story within its broader philosophical framework.

variants: the sword hanging by a thread
